author: @usernamesreprise4068
text: I KNEW you were going to say you fed the wire through by pulling the trigger and holding it, - but fun fact that isnt mentioned in the teutonic englander manual is, see that little red button  above the wire feed tensioner handle, THAT is a wire feed motor  button for feeding the wire through without using the torch trigger which ALSO makes the wire live while it is feeding and also wastes the gas if using the gas set up, which you really ought to try to use soon, the resulting welds are FAR superior, you had the amperage set slightly too high  evidenced in the amount of spatter you had all over the peice try doing it again with the amperage gain lowered a little, and to get the fish scale effect you were looking for  feed the wire in straight and using the tiniest movements draw it backwards  pushing into and out of the weld pool as you move down the peice

author: @matthewellisor5835
text:
I'm no expert; barely adequate is all that I hope for. Just throwing out there a few bits that I've found helpful.
Relax! Make sure that you're in a comfortable position and have stretched out shaken out any muscle knots. Also, pull your head out of the fumes a bit, you don't need to be nose-deep into the puddle. Last, if it sounds like bacon, brother, you're weldin'. If it smells like bacon, mate! You're on fire!!

author: @TheWascallyWelder.1
text: You don't need to whip the wire back and forth like you do with SMAW just let the wire do the work just concentrate on running a straight bead try some overhead and uphill welding as well but watch someone else's overhead welding videos so you get your body position right and travel direction correct so you don't burn your back, arms and neck up trying to weld overhead

author: @edwardhughes352
text: Nice

author: @masshomic1dex10
text: The wire tensioner should pull towards you, which will open the rollers, that way you dont need to reset your tension everytime! :)

author: @MrAnderson4509
text: Luke, Wait until you try out a Tig setup, your detail will be awesome with the detail you want, and then there is the aluminium and stainless steel, thin sheet metal will be a piece of cake!

author: @ArtisticImpressionsbyBobRouth
text: Do they make an aluminum resin core wire too?   On your test welds you might want to grind the weld flat and see if its got pits or gaps.  Final exam in welding class ment that it had to pass the stretch test to see how strong the weld is.

author: @AtlasReburdened
text: You need that thing running a bit hotter or you need to slow the feed, as indicated by some parts of your weld overhanging the base material. I would recommend slowing the feed and the travel speed of your weld.

author: @docink6175
text: I always thought MIG welding stood for metal insert gas welding, how can you mig weld w/o gas? or this is an ARC/MIG machine? It is good to see you expanding your knowledge base, keep practicing and you will get better and more comfortable with it

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: It's just flux core. The wire contains a core of flux. The welds are kind of a compromise between stick and mig. They can be pretty nice if you're good.

author: @gavinstirling7088
text: Flux core is great for welding galvanized steel, not so great for very thin sheet metal though.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Awesome! flux core almost feels like controlled ark welding to me lol, time to get some gas and weld some really thin stuff! Thank you mate! :)

author: @dexterconnolly3696
text: My test for welding at mit was to make a 10cm cube from 2mm steel and weld a nut on one side to pressure test the welds after.

author: @dexterconnolly3696
text: Great video Luke. Practice and patience makes perfect. I was thinking would graphene work as a wire? If so it would be possible to 3d print coils made of graphene for eletcric motors.just a thought. Keep it up bro

author: @theimpatientbrewer
text: I found that for the thinner sheet metal, you need to use 0.6 mm wire and argon/CO2 gas. The flux core is too aggressive.

replies.author: @gavinstirling7088
replies.text: Good call, I've recently found this out too whilst trying to weld a car body panel with 0.8mm flux core which ended up blowing through even on the lowest current setting. It's possible to do very short spot welds if you're desperate though.

replies.author: @masshomic1dex10
replies.text:
I currently use 0.9mm wire for anything ranging from 1.55mm sheet to basically any rhs/shs or fms, mild or galvanised.
As long as you adjust your settings accordingly you should be fine, if your blowing holes too easily your running it too hot. Also good idea to stitch sheet first to stop any warping

author: @allanwood3562
text:
Ok so now it pay you to break it open and inspect the weld cross section.
Maybe pad welding a plate will give you a lot of control practice very quickly too. Good luck and enjoy.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: Yes, I should have used a grinder to grind the welds down to see if there were any pits, and to test the strengh. I will do this next time for sure. Thank you mate! :)

author: @totherarf
text:
Great Video Luke, but .... A word of warning!

You don't need to get up so close and personal when you weld. Doing it the way you did means you are breathing that smoke in! There is a good reason there are few old stick welders ..... that smoke is more toxic than one of Rob's Woodbines!

If you are doing it inside a good fan can work wonders (as well as a grinder and paint)! ;o)

replies.author: @AtlasReburdened
replies.text: This point can't be stressed enough. Old stick welders are a very rare breed.

author: @mikebond6328
text: Manufacturers tell you that you don’t need shielding gas with flux-cored wire but you’ll never be happy with your welds if you don’t use a shielding gas even with flux-cored wire.

replies.author: @TheWascallyWelder.1
replies.text: That is absolutely untrue I have never been disappointed with any of my straight fluxcor welding, now on the other hand I absolutely wouldn't weld with dual-shielded  wire without gas.

author: @javiermorton7170
text: Thanks for another good video.

author: @silverclouds3725
text: Great vid - thanks for having us along for the ride!  =o)

author: @stevetobias4890
text:
Only way to improve is with practice. I have never welded before but I will maybe try if I can get gloves to fit without making things hard to grip.
Great effort Luke!

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: For sure! I am planning on using this much more. I agree with the gloves LOL I can never get them to feel comfortable hahaha. Thank you mate! :)

author: @jgfugfljouyf8374
text: I love my mig welder

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: I loving this setup as well! Just have to get some gas now lol. Thanks! :)

author: @jvon3885
text: Next you will want to get into Tig. My first test at the Covington Cycle City shop was to weld two soda cans together without any blowouts.

replies.author: @MyNameIsLukey
replies.text: @jvon3885 That is an awesome test, that will get you used to welding thin metals for sure. I might have to try this. Thanks mate! :)

author: @karlmyers6518
text: Definitely your best video yet. Might be biased because I had a shout out. Get Rob to treat you to a big bottle of gas if you really want good looking welds . Also try to relax a bit more and pull your head out of the smoke a bit mate.
